summary: Solid TDD work. Tests are behavioral and cover the right surface area. Implementation is minimal and follows existing patterns exactly. Two minor issues: the as-cast for PushResult is unverified at runtime (pre-existing pattern), and the createApiClient smoke test was not updated to include push.

suggestions.description: Unsafe type assertion: request() returns Promise<unknown>, cast to Promise<PushResult> without runtime validation
suggestions.rationale: If the server returns a response that does not match PushResult (e.g. missing fields, wrong types), the code silently accepts it. This is a pre-existing pattern shared by getBoard/getFeed, but it means the type system is lying about what push() actually returns.
suggestions.suggestion: Consider adding a runtime validation function (e.g. a type guard or schema validator) for PushResult before returning. This would close the gap between the runtime value and the TypeScript type. However, since this is a pre-existing pattern across all methods, fixing it for push alone would be inconsistent -- it should be addressed holistically.

suggestions.description: createApiClient smoke test does not verify push method exists
suggestions.rationale: The existing test at line 35-47 checks that all methods are functions (getBoard, getFeed, createEpic, etc.) but was not updated to include push. If push were accidentally removed, this smoke test would not catch it.
suggestions.suggestion: Add expect(client.push).toBeTypeOf("function") to the createApiClient smoke test.

suggestions.description: Auth header test for push is redundant with shared auth header tests
suggestions.rationale: The auth header describe block (lines 50-89) already proves that Bearer token is sent for both GET and POST requests via the shared request() function. The push-specific auth test exercises the same code path and adds no new coverage.
suggestions.suggestion: This is a judgment call. The test is not wrong, and some teams prefer per-method auth verification for defense in depth. But it is strictly redundant with the existing auth header tests.
